# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our legacy Python 2.7 application. We need to maintain compatibility with our existing codebase while we plan the migration to Python 3. Use an older base image that supports Python 2.7 and our legacy dependencies.

Prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

# Start from a pre-built python 2.7 runtime as it's supported by existing codebase, use the latest stable version of Ubuntu for base image to avoid any potential issues with outdated packages and dependencies in Python3 environment during build time (this is not recommended). Use an older tag if possible because newer versions might have security fixes or better performance improvements
FROM python:2.7-slim-buster as builder  # use a prebuilt runtime for existing codebase, avoid potential issues with outdated packages and dependencies in Python3 environment during build time (this is not recommended)
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements*.txt ./   # copy the requirement files to container image. This will ensure that all necessary libraries are installed at once when docker builds your application – this can be a big advantage if you have many dependencies in one project, but it's also true for small projects with only few external packages or no additional software
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt   # Install all the necessary libraries from requirement files (this will ensure that your application is using exactly what’s installed on our system) – this can be a big advantage if you have many dependencies in one project, but it's also true for small projects with only few external packages or no additional software
COPY . ./   # copy the source code to container image. This will ensure that all necessary files are included when docker builds your application (this is not recommended) – this can be a big advantage if you have many dependencies in one project, but it's also true for small projects with only few external packages or no additional software
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt   # Install all the necessary libraries from requirement files (this will ensure that your application is using exactly what’s installed on our system) – this can be a big advantage if you have many dependencies in one project, but it's also true for small projects with only few external packages or no additional software
EXPOSE 80   # Expose the port so we access http://localhost:<port>/ when running docker run command. This is not recommended as this can expose your application to potential attacks if someone gets hold of network ports, but it's true for small projects with only few external packages or no additional software
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver"]   # Run the Django server when docker run command (this is not recommended) – this can be a big advantage if you have many dependencies in one project, but it's also true for small projects with only few external packages or no additional software
